title: DSA Cheatsheet
description: Data Structures & Algorithms explained intuitively — learn to think like an engineer, not just memorize patterns

concepts.name: Time Complexity Basics
concepts.explanation: Big O tells you 'how badly does this slow down when my input grows?' Think of it as: O(1) is instant (grabbing the first book), O(n) is reading every book on a shelf, O(n²) is comparing every book with every other book. The key insight: focus on what happens when n gets HUGE.
keyPoints: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) — memorize this order!
Drop constants: O(5n) → O(n) because 5x slower is still linear
Drop smaller terms: O(n² + n) → O(n²) — n² dominates when n is huge
See nested loops? Probably O(n²). See halving? Probably O(log n).
Interview tip: always state your complexity when presenting solutions

concepts.name: Space Complexity
concepts.explanation: Space complexity = how much extra memory does your algorithm rent? Imagine your apartment (memory). O(1) means you only need your keys (constant). O(n) means you need a box for every item you're processing. Recursion sneakily uses memory too — each call is like stacking plates that need to be unstacked.
keyPoints: Space = extra memory used (not counting the input itself)
O(1) = in-place, just a few variables — best for memory!
O(n) = creating arrays, HashMaps, or deep recursion
Recursion depth = space used (each call lives on the stack)
Trade-off: sometimes using more space gives you faster time

concepts.name: Complexity Comparison Table
concepts.explanation: Common time complexities and their performance characteristics.
keyPoints: O(1) - Hash table lookup, array access
O(log n) - Binary search, balanced tree ops
O(n) - Linear search, single pass
O(n log n) - Efficient sorting (merge, heap)
O(n²) - Nested loops, bubble sort
O(2^n) - Recursive subsets, brute force

concepts.name: Array Basics & Operations
concepts.explanation: Think of an array like a parking lot with numbered spots. You can instantly jump to spot #500 (O(1) access!), but if you want to insert a car in the middle, everyone after that spot has to move over (O(n) insert). Fixed size means you can't add more spots once built.
keyPoints: Array = fixed-size, numbered slots (like a parking lot)
Access by index = O(1) — this is arrays' superpower!
Insert/delete in middle = O(n) — everything shifts
Index starts at 0, last index = length - 1
Need dynamic size? Use ArrayList instead

concepts.name: Two Pointer Technique
concepts.explanation: Imagine two people searching a sorted bookshelf — one starts at the beginning, one at the end. If the sum of their books is too small, the left person moves right (bigger numbers). Too big? Right person moves left. This 'pincer movement' turns O(n²) problems into O(n). Magic!
keyPoints: Two pointers: opposite ends moving inward, or same direction at different speeds
Works best on SORTED arrays — sorting first is okay if problem allows
Turns O(n²) brute force into O(n) — huge improvement!
Key patterns: two sum, container water, palindrome check, remove duplicates
Ask yourself: 'Can I eliminate options by comparing ends?'

concepts.name: Sliding Window
concepts.explanation: Imagine you're on a train looking through a window at houses. Instead of counting all houses from scratch each time you move, you just subtract the house that left your view and add the new one that entered. That's sliding window — maintain a 'window' of elements and slide it efficiently.
keyPoints: Fixed window: size stays constant, just slide along
Variable window: expand right, shrink left when condition breaks
The trick: don't recalculate everything — just update the edges!
Common patterns: max sum subarray, longest substring, minimum window
Time complexity: O(n) because each element is added and removed at most once

concepts.name: Kadane's Algorithm
concepts.explanation: Find maximum sum contiguous subarray in O(n) time.
keyPoints: Classic DP problem solved in O(n)
At each position: include current or start fresh
currentSum tracks running sum
maxSum tracks best sum seen so far
Handles all negative numbers correctly

concepts.name: Dutch National Flag (3-way Partitioning)
concepts.explanation: Sort array with 3 distinct values in single pass.
keyPoints: Three pointers: low, mid, high
low tracks end of 0s region
high tracks start of 2s region
mid explores unknown region
Single pass O(n) solution

concepts.name: Common Array Problems
concepts.explanation: Frequently asked array manipulation problems.
keyPoints: Rotate: reverse whole, reverse parts
Missing number: use sum formula or XOR
Duplicate: Floyd's cycle detection
Merge: two-pointer technique
Practice: Stock buy/sell, majority element

concepts.name: String Basics & Manipulation
concepts.explanation: Strings are immutable in Java - use StringBuilder for modifications.
keyPoints: Strings are immutable - concat creates new string
Use StringBuilder for multiple modifications
equals() for content, == for reference
charAt() is O(1), substring() is O(n)
String pool saves memory for literals

concepts.name: Palindrome Check
concepts.explanation: Check if string reads same forwards and backwards.
keyPoints: Two-pointer from both ends
Skip non-alphanumeric for valid palindrome
Expand around center for longest palindrome
Consider both odd and even length palindromes
Manacher's algorithm for O(n) solution

concepts.name: Anagram Detection
concepts.explanation: Check if two strings have same characters with same frequency.
keyPoints: Anagrams have same character frequencies
Sorting approach: O(n log n) time
Frequency array: O(n) time, O(1) space
Use HashMap to group anagrams
Frequency array as key for grouping

concepts.name: String Reversal & Rotation
concepts.explanation: Reverse string or its parts, check rotation.
keyPoints: Use char array for in-place reversal
Split and reverse for word reversal
Rotation check: s2 in s1+s1
Efficient rotation using substring
Consider edge cases: empty, single char

concepts.name: Pattern Matching & Substrings
concepts.explanation: Find patterns, substrings, and repetitions.
keyPoints: KMP for efficient pattern matching O(n+m)
Sliding window for substring problems
LPS array for KMP preprocessing
indexOf() for simple pattern search
Consider edge cases: empty strings

concepts.name: Singly Linked List Implementation
concepts.explanation: A linked list is like a treasure hunt — each clue (node) tells you where the next clue is. Unlike arrays, there are no numbered spots; you have to follow the chain. Great for inserting/deleting (just rewire pointers!), but terrible for 'give me the 50th element' (you have to walk through 50 nodes).
keyPoints: Linked List = chain of nodes, each pointing to the next
Insert/Delete at head = O(1) — just change one pointer!
Insert/Delete at end = O(n) — must walk the whole list
No random access like arrays — finding nth element is O(n)
Great when you need frequent insertions/deletions

concepts.name: Reverse Linked List
concepts.explanation: Reverse the direction of all pointers in the list.
keyPoints: Iterative uses 3 pointers: prev, current, next
Recursive reverses from end to start
Iterative is space-efficient O(1)
Recursive uses O(n) stack space
Common interview question

concepts.name: Detect Cycle (Floyd's Algorithm)
concepts.explanation: The Tortoise and Hare race! Send two runners around the track — one moves 1 step, the other moves 2 steps. If there's a loop, the fast one will eventually lap the slow one and they'll meet. If there's no loop, the fast one will hit the end. Bonus: to find WHERE the loop starts, reset one to head and move both at same speed until they meet again.
keyPoints: Floyd's Algorithm = Tortoise and Hare (slow/fast pointers)
If they meet → cycle exists. If fast hits null → no cycle
To find cycle start: reset slow to head, move both 1 step until they meet
Why it works: math! Distance traveled relates to cycle entry point
O(n) time, O(1) space — no extra data structures needed!

concepts.name: Find Middle & Merge Lists
concepts.explanation: Find middle element and merge two sorted lists.
keyPoints: Middle: slow-fast pointer technique
Merge: use dummy node for simplicity
Merge K lists: use min-heap (PriorityQueue)
Palindrome: find middle, reverse, compare
Dummy node simplifies edge cases

concepts.name: Doubly Linked List
concepts.explanation: Each node has references to both next and previous nodes.
keyPoints: Each node has prev and next pointers
Allows bidirectional traversal
Delete with node reference: O(1)
Extra space for prev pointer
Useful for LRU cache, browser history

concepts.name: Stack Implementation
concepts.explanation: A stack is like a pile of plates — you can only add or remove from the top. Last plate in is the first plate out (LIFO). This simple rule makes stacks perfect for 'undo' operations, tracking function calls, and parsing brackets. Every operation is O(1) because you're always working at the top!
keyPoints: LIFO = Last In, First Out (like plates in a cafeteria)
All operations O(1) — push, pop, peek, isEmpty
Use Deque (ArrayDeque) in Java, not the legacy Stack class
Perfect for: undo/redo, bracket matching, DFS, expression parsing
Interview pattern: 'track something I'll need later' = stack

concepts.name: Balanced Parentheses
concepts.explanation: Check if parentheses/brackets are properly balanced.
keyPoints: Push opening brackets onto stack
Pop and match for closing brackets
Stack must be empty at end
Use HashMap for cleaner matching
Track indices for removal problems

concepts.name: Next Greater Element
concepts.explanation: Find next greater element for each element in array.
keyPoints: Traverse from right to left
Pop smaller/equal elements
Stack stores potential candidates
O(n) time - each element pushed/popped once
Stock span uses same concept

concepts.name: Min Stack & Expression Evaluation
concepts.explanation: Stack with O(1) getMin operation and expression evaluation.
keyPoints: MinStack: use auxiliary stack for minimums
Postfix: operands to stack, operators compute
Infix→Postfix: use precedence
Parentheses have highest precedence
All O(n) time and space

concepts.name: Histogram & Trapping Water
concepts.explanation: Find largest rectangle in histogram and trap rainwater.
keyPoints: Histogram: stack stores increasing heights
Pop when current < stack top
Calculate area using popped height
Trap water: bound by min of left/right max
Two-pointer approach for O(1) space

concepts.name: Queue Implementation
concepts.explanation: FIFO (First In First Out) data structure for sequential processing.
keyPoints: FIFO: First In First Out
Enqueue O(1), Dequeue O(1)
Use LinkedList or ArrayDeque
Circular queue prevents waste
Common in: BFS, scheduling, buffering

concepts.name: Priority Queue (Heap)
concepts.explanation: Queue where elements are served based on priority, not order.
keyPoints: Implemented using heap (min or max)
offer() and poll() are O(log n)
Default is min-heap in Java
Custom comparator for max-heap
Used in: Dijkstra, A*, top K problems

concepts.name: Deque (Double-Ended Queue)
concepts.explanation: Queue allowing insertion/deletion from both ends.
keyPoints: Can act as stack or queue
All operations O(1)
Preferred over Stack class
Sliding window maximum uses deque
Stores indices for window problems

concepts.name: Binary Tree Basics
concepts.explanation: A tree is like a family tree turned upside down — one ancestor (root) at the top, each person (node) has at most 2 children. No cycles, no shared parents. The key insight: most tree problems are solved with recursion — 'process this node, then do the same for left and right'. Master the 3 traversal orders and you'll nail 80% of tree problems.
keyPoints: INORDER = Left, Root, Right (BST → sorted output!)
PREORDER = Root, Left, Right (for copying trees)
POSTORDER = Left, Right, Root (for deleting trees)
LEVEL ORDER = BFS with queue (for shortest path in trees)
Recursion pattern: base case (null), process node, recurse children

concepts.name: Binary Search Tree (BST)
concepts.explanation: Binary tree where left < root < right for each node.
keyPoints: Left subtree < node < right subtree
Inorder gives sorted sequence
Average O(log n), worst O(n) if skewed
Delete: find inorder successor/predecessor
Validate using min-max range

concepts.name: Tree Problems
concepts.explanation: Common tree problems and their solutions.
keyPoints: Depth/Height: recursively check children
Balanced: height difference ≤ 1
Diameter: longest path between any two nodes
Path sum: DFS with running sum
Serialize: preorder with null markers

concepts.name: Trie (Prefix Tree)
concepts.explanation: Tree for efficient string storage and prefix searching.
keyPoints: Efficient for prefix matching - O(m)
Space: O(ALPHABET_SIZE * m * n)
Each node has array of children
isEndOfWord marks complete words
Use cases: autocomplete, spell check

concepts.name: Basic Sorting (O(n²))
concepts.explanation: Simple sorting algorithms - Bubble, Selection, Insertion Sort.
keyPoints: Bubble: swap adjacent if out of order
Selection: find min and swap
Insertion: insert in sorted portion
All O(n²) but insertion fastest for small/sorted
In-place, stable (bubble, insertion)

concepts.name: Merge Sort & Quick Sort
concepts.explanation: Efficient O(n log n) divide and conquer sorting algorithms.
keyPoints: Merge: Stable, O(n) space, predictable O(n log n)
Quick: In-place, O(log n) space, average O(n log n)
Quick sort worst case O(n²) with bad pivot
Merge better for linked lists
Quick sort preferred in practice

concepts.name: Heap Sort & Counting Sort
concepts.explanation: Heap sort and non-comparison based counting sort.
keyPoints: Heap: In-place, not stable, O(n log n) guaranteed
Counting: Non-comparison, O(n+k), for integers
Counting requires knowing range
Counting is stable
Heap sort: build heap then extract max

concepts.name: Linear & Binary Search
concepts.explanation: Basic searching algorithms for arrays.
keyPoints: Linear: works on unsorted, O(n)
Binary: requires sorted array, O(log n)
Binary: divide search space in half
Use left + (right - left) / 2 to avoid overflow
Binary search is base for many algorithms

concepts.name: Binary Search Variants
concepts.explanation: Advanced binary search problems - first, last, closest element.
keyPoints: First occurrence: search left after finding
Last occurrence: search right after finding
Peak element: compare with neighbor
Rotated array: determine sorted half
All maintain O(log n) complexity

concepts.name: DP Fundamentals
concepts.explanation: DP is just 'smart recursion' — instead of solving the same subproblem 100 times, solve it once and save the answer. Think of it like a GPS that remembers routes it already calculated. Two approaches: TOP-DOWN (memoization) starts from the goal and caches results, BOTTOM-UP (tabulation) builds answers from smallest subproblems up.
keyPoints: DP = Recursion + Caching (save answers to avoid re-solving)
Memoization = top-down (recursive with cache)
Tabulation = bottom-up (iterative, fill table)
The pattern: 1) Define state 2) Find recurrence 3) Identify base cases
Space optimization: often you only need the last 1-2 values, not the whole array

concepts.name: 0/1 Knapsack & Subset Sum
concepts.explanation: Classic DP problems involving item selection.
keyPoints: Knapsack: include or exclude each item
dp[i][w] = max value with i items, capacity w
Subset sum: boolean DP for existence
Partition: subset sum with target = total/2
Can optimize space to O(W) or O(sum)

concepts.name: Longest Common Subsequence
concepts.explanation: Find longest common subsequence between two strings.
keyPoints: LCS: if match, add 1 to diagonal
If no match, take max of left/top
Backtrack to print actual LCS
LIS: dp[i] = longest ending at i
LIS can be optimized to O(n log n)

concepts.name: Coin Change & Edit Distance
concepts.explanation: Classic DP problems for interviews.
keyPoints: Coin change: dp[i] = min coins for amount i
Ways: order matters vs doesn't matter
Edit distance: insert, delete, replace
Base case: empty string needs i/j operations
Match: no operation needed

concepts.name: Graph Representation
concepts.explanation: Different ways to represent graphs in code.
keyPoints: Adjacency List: Best for sparse graphs
Adjacency Matrix: Best for dense graphs
List: O(V+E) space, O(V) edge lookup
Matrix: O(V²) space, O(1) edge lookup
Weighted graphs store weight with edge

concepts.name: BFS & DFS Traversal
concepts.explanation: BFS (Breadth-First Search) is like ripples in a pond — explore all neighbors at distance 1, then distance 2, etc. Uses a QUEUE. DFS (Depth-First Search) is like exploring a maze by going as far as possible down one path before backtracking. Uses a STACK (or recursion). BFS finds shortest path in unweighted graphs. DFS is simpler and uses less memory.
keyPoints: BFS = Queue + visit level by level (water ripples)
DFS = Stack/Recursion + go deep first (maze exploration)
BFS finds SHORTEST PATH in unweighted graphs
DFS uses less memory (just the current path)
Both are O(V + E) time — you visit each vertex and edge once

concepts.name: Cycle Detection
concepts.explanation: Detect cycles in directed and undirected graphs.
keyPoints: Undirected: back edge to visited (not parent)
Directed: use recursion stack
recStack tracks current path
Union-Find also works for undirected
O(V+E) time complexity

concepts.name: Dijkstra's Shortest Path
concepts.explanation: Find shortest path from source to all vertices.
keyPoints: Greedy algorithm for shortest path
Works with non-negative weights only
Use priority queue (min-heap)
O((V+E) log V) with binary heap
Cannot handle negative weights

concepts.name: Topological Sort
concepts.explanation: Linear ordering of vertices in directed acyclic graph (DAG).
keyPoints: Only for DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs)
DFS: post-order traversal
Kahn's: remove nodes with inDegree 0
Can detect cycles (result size < V)
Used in: task scheduling, build systems

concepts.name: HashMap & HashSet Basics
concepts.explanation: A HashMap is like a magical library where you shout a book title and it appears instantly — O(1) lookup! It works by converting keys to numbers (hashing) that point to storage locations. HashSet is the same idea but only stores unique values (no duplicates). These are your go-to structures when you need fast lookups.
keyPoints: HashMap = key-value pairs, O(1) for get/put/remove
HashSet = unique values only, O(1) for add/contains/remove
Always use getOrDefault() to avoid null checks
TreeMap = sorted keys (O(log n)), LinkedHashMap = insertion order
Interview hack: 'need fast lookup?' → HashMap/HashSet

concepts.name: Hashing Problems
concepts.explanation: Common problems solved using hashing.
keyPoints: Hash map for O(1) lookups
Use complement for two sum
Sorted string as key for anagrams
HashSet for sequence checking
Prefix sum with HashMap for subarray

concepts.name: Permutations & Combinations
concepts.explanation: Generate all permutations and combinations.
keyPoints: Backtrack: explore, then undo
Permutations: use all elements
Combinations: select k elements
Subsets: all possible combinations
Always copy list when adding to result

concepts.name: N-Queens & Sudoku
concepts.explanation: Classic backtracking problems.
keyPoints: N-Queens: place queens row by row
Check column and diagonals for validity
Sudoku: try each number 1-9
Validate row, column, 3x3 box
Backtrack if no solution found

concepts.name: Activity Selection & Intervals
concepts.explanation: Greedy approach for interval scheduling problems.
keyPoints: Sort by end time for activity selection
Greedy: always pick earliest ending
Merge intervals: sort by start time
Meeting rooms: track concurrent meetings
Local optimal leads to global optimal

concepts.name: Fractional Knapsack & Huffman
concepts.explanation: Classic greedy algorithms.
keyPoints: Fractional: sort by value/weight ratio
Can take fraction of items
Jump game: track maximum reachable
Greedy choice at each step
Prove greedy choice is optimal

concepts.name: Bitwise Operations
concepts.explanation: Essential bit manipulation techniques.
keyPoints: & (AND): check bits, clear bits
| (OR): set bits
^ (XOR): toggle bits, find unique
<< left shift: multiply by 2
>> right shift: divide by 2
n & (n-1): clear rightmost set bit

concepts.name: Bit Manipulation Problems
concepts.explanation: Common bit manipulation interview problems.
keyPoints: XOR properties: a^a=0, a^0=a
XOR all to find unique element
Use rightmost bit to separate groups
Reverse bits: process each bit
Generate subsets: 2^n iterations

concepts.name: GCD, LCM & Prime Numbers
concepts.explanation: Fundamental mathematical algorithms.
keyPoints: GCD: Euclidean algorithm is efficient
LCM: (a*b) / GCD(a,b)
Prime check: test up to √n
Sieve: efficient for multiple primes
Sieve: mark multiples as composite

concepts.name: Power & Modular Arithmetic
concepts.explanation: Fast exponentiation and modular operations.
keyPoints: Fast power: O(log n) instead of O(n)
Modular arithmetic prevents overflow
nCr: use min(r, n-r) for efficiency
Pascal's: each number is sum of two above

concepts.name: Pattern Recognition Guide
concepts.explanation: The secret to coding interviews: recognize the PATTERN, not the problem. Most problems fit into a handful of patterns. Train yourself to spot these clues early — it's like identifying recipes by the ingredients.
keyPoints: Read the problem → Identify keywords → Match to pattern
"Optimal" usually means there's a better approach than brute force
Most array problems: Two Pointers, Sliding Window, or HashMap
Most tree problems: Recursion (pre/in/postorder) or BFS
When stuck: What data structure gives me O(1) lookup for what I need?

concepts.name: Common Interview Gotchas
concepts.explanation: The bugs that trip up 90% of candidates. Memorize these edge cases and checks — they show interviewers you think like a real engineer, not just someone who memorized solutions.
keyPoints: ALWAYS check: null, empty, single element cases first
Arrays: watch for index out of bounds (arr[i-1], arr[i+1])
Strings: consider empty string, single char, spaces
Integers: consider 0, negative, very large numbers (overflow)
Think out loud — interviewers can only help if they know your thinking

concepts.name: Data Structure Selection
concepts.explanation: Choosing the right data structure is half the battle. Each structure has superpowers and weaknesses. Match the problem's needs to the structure's strengths.
keyPoints: Fast lookup by value → HashSet
Fast lookup by key → HashMap
Maintain sorted order → TreeMap/TreeSet
Track min/max → PriorityQueue (Heap)
Random access by index → ArrayList
